article_title: Ransomware accidentally destroys all files larger than 128KB, preventing decryption — VECT code likely partly vibe coded with AI or used an old code base, security researchers suggest
core_thesis: The VECT ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) contains a critical programming flaw that turns it into a wiper for files larger than 128KB, making decryption impossible even with the attacker's key. Check Point Research (CPR) attributes the bug to poor coding practices, likely from AI-generated code or an outdated codebase, and warns that despite the flaw, the group's ambition and existing distribution network pose a future threat.
detailed_summary:
VECT ransomware, first observed in December 2025, was designed as a RaaS targeting Windows, Linux, and ESXi systems. However, CPR discovered a catastrophic bug: for files larger than 128KB, the ransomware splits the file into four chunks and encrypts each with a random 12-byte nonce. All four nonces are written to the same shared output buffer, causing each new nonce to overwrite the previous one. Only the last nonce is preserved, so decryption of the first three chunks is impossible. This effectively turns the ransomware into a wiper, as victims cannot recover their data even if they pay the ransom.

Additional flaws include improper CPU thread usage, self-canceling string obfuscation routines, misidentified ciphers in public reports, and a non-functional encryption method selection (fast, medium, secure) that is parsed but never implemented. The malware also incorrectly lists Ukraine as a CIS member, an outdated geopolitical stance. CPR suggests the code was likely 'vibe coded' using AI or derived from an old codebase, given the amateurish mistakes. Despite these issues, the group has built a distribution network via BreachForums and partnered with other threat actors, indicating potential for future, more effective versions.

entities.name: VECT
entities.role: Ransomware-as-a-service group behind the flawed ransomware

entities.name: Check Point Research (CPR)
entities.role: Cybersecurity firm that discovered and analyzed the VECT ransomware bug

entities.name: TeamPCP
entities.role: Threat actor group partnered with VECT

entities.name: BreachForums
entities.role: Online forum used by VECT to build its affiliate network

entities.name: Nitrogen ransomware
entities.role: Another ransomware group that made a similar encryption mistake earlier in 2026

key_data_points: VECT ransomware splits files larger than 128KB into four chunks and encrypts each with a random 12-byte nonce, but all nonces are written to the same buffer, causing overwrites. Only the last nonce is saved, making decryption of the first three chunks impossible. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This flaw can be weaponized by defenders to demonstrate the risks of poor ransomware coding, or by attackers to understand common pitfalls in encryption implementation.)

The ransomware offers three encryption methods (fast, medium, secure) but the selection is parsed and never implemented, meaning all files are encrypted with the same method regardless of choice. (🟢 Relevant to programming: highlights the importance of verifying that all code paths are functional, especially in security-critical software.)

CPR found self-canceling string obfuscation routines, misidentified ciphers, and improper CPU thread usage, indicating amateurish development likely from AI-generated code or an old codebase. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: such flaws can be exploited to detect or disrupt ransomware operations.)

VECT incorrectly includes Ukraine as a CIS member, a geopolitical error most ransomware groups corrected after 2022. (🟢 Relevant to cybersecurity: shows the group may be inexperienced or using outdated templates.)

Despite the bug, VECT has multi-platform capabilities (Windows, Linux, ESXi), partnerships with TeamPCP, and an affiliate network on BreachForums, indicating ambition and potential for future improved versions. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: the group's infrastructure and distribution methods are worth monitoring for future threats.)
conclusions: The VECT ransomware's critical nonce overwrite bug renders it ineffective for extortion on files larger than 128KB, as decryption is impossible even with the key. This demonstrates that poor coding practices, possibly from AI assistance, can backfire on attackers. However, the group's existing distribution network and ambition suggest they may release a fixed version, making them a future threat. For Red Teamers, this case study highlights the importance of understanding encryption implementation flaws and the risks of relying on AI-generated code without thorough review.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming and programming interests. It provides a real-world example of a ransomware implementation failure due to a buffer overwrite bug, which you can study to understand common encryption pitfalls and how to avoid them in your own tools. The analysis of AI-generated code ('vibe coding') offers a cautionary tale about relying on AI without verification. As a student, you can use this case to deepen your understanding of ransomware internals, nonce management, and the importance of robust coding practices. The article also underscores that even amateurish groups can build distribution networks, so monitoring such threats is valuable for your cybersecurity awareness.
extra_advice: Consider reverse-engineering the VECT ransomware sample (if available) to observe the nonce overwrite bug firsthand. This will sharpen your skills in malware analysis and encryption flaw detection. Additionally, experiment with implementing a secure file encryption routine in your own projects, ensuring proper nonce handling and buffer management, to solidify your understanding.
